tex: Shade one square so that the diagram has 1 line of symmetry.

Put **one** pair of brackets into this calculation to make it correct.

\[
80 \div 8 + 2 \times 3 = 24
\]

Tim drives home from work.

The travel graph shows his journey.

(a) 
t Write down the time Tim leaves work.

t Tim stops on the way home.

(i)  Find how far Tim travels before he stops.

 Find how long Tim stops for.

Calculate.

\[
\frac{2.8^5 - 0.3^2}{\sqrt{5}}
\]

Give your answer correct to 2 decimal places.

In a bag of counters, 6 of the counters are blue.

The arrow ($\downarrow$) on the probability scale shows the probability of picking a blue counter at random.

Work out the total number of counters in the bag.

The local time in Japan is $3\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours ahead of the local time in India.

Find the time in Japan when it is 21\,45 in India.

Mia changes \$350 into euros.

The exchange rate is \$1 = 0.92 euros.

Calculate the amount Mia receives.

$ABC$ is a straight line.

$BD$ is parallel to $AE$.

(a) 
t Find the value of $x$.

Give a geometrical reason for your answer.

$x = $ ... because ...

t Find the value of $y$.

Give a geometrical reason for your answer.

$y = $ ... because ...

(a) 
t Expand and simplify.

\[
5(x - 2) + 3(x - 7)
\]

t Factorise.

\[
4a^2 + 16a
\]

Kai opens his restaurant from Tuesday to Saturday each week.

He counts the number of customers each day for two weeks.

The bar chart shows some of the results.

(a) 
t In week 1 there were 200 customers in total.

Complete the bar chart.

t Write down the mode for week 2.

t Find the mean number of customers for the 5 days in week 2.

In May a shop makes a profit of \$542.

In June the shop makes a profit of \$600.

(a) 
t Calculate the percentage increase in the profit from May to June.

t The shop gives $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of the profit from May to a charity.

Find the amount the shop gives to the charity.

t The shop spends 38\% of the profit from June on a fridge.

Find the amount the shop spends on the fridge.

Meg invests \$2500 at a rate of 4.6\% per year simple interest.

Calculate the total amount of her investment at the end of 3 years.

The table gives information about the costs of hiring bikes.

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
Type of bike & Cost for first day & Cost for each extra day \\
\hline
Road & \$25 & \$20 \\
\hline
Mountain & \$40 & \$35 \\
\hline
Electric & \$70 & \$50 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(a) 
t Work out the cost of hiring 2 road bikes for 3 days.

t The cost, $M$, of hiring a mountain bike for $d$ days can be written as $M = 35d + 5$.

(i)  Write a formula for the cost, $E$, of hiring an electric bike for $d$ days.

 The cost of hiring an electric bike for 6 days is the same as the cost of hiring a mountain bike for $d$ days.

Find the value of $d$.
texHtml:
The table gives information about the costs of hiring bikes.
Type of bikeCost for first dayCost for each extra day
Road\$25\$20
Mountain\$40\$35
Electric\$70\$50
(a) t Work out the cost of hiring 2 road bikes for 3 days. t The cost, $M$, of hiring a mountain bike for $d$ days can be written as $M = 35d + 5$. (i) Write a formula for the cost, $E$, of hiring an electric bike for $d$ days. The cost of hiring an electric bike for 6 days is the same as the cost of hiring a mountain bike for $d$ days. Find the value of $d$.

tex:
A spinner is numbered 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5.

Rinesh spins the spinner many times.

He works out the relative frequency that the spinner lands on each number.

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
Number & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
Relative frequency & 0.15 & 0.22 & & 0.26 & 0.25 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(a) 
t Complete the table.

t Rinesh spins the spinner another 1500 times.

Calculate the expected number of times the spinner lands on 2.
texHtml:
A spinner is numbered 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. Rinesh spins the spinner many times. He works out the relative frequency that the spinner lands on each number.
Number12345
Relative frequency0.150.220.260.25

tex:

t These are the distances above the surface of the Earth of five satellites, $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ and $E$.

Each distance is in kilometres.

$A$ & $B$ & $C$ & $D$ & $E$ \\
35\,800 & $7.8 \times 10^2$ & $1.5 \times 10^6$ & 535 & $2 \times 10^4$ \\
\end{tabular}
(i)  Write these distances in order, starting with the shortest.

... , ... , ... , ... , ...

 \textit{shortest}

 The radius of Earth is 6370\,km.

Satellite $A$ is $k$ times further from the **centre** of Earth than satellite $D$.

Show that $k = 6.11$ correct to 2 decimal places.

t A satellite travels at a speed of 27\,000\,km/h.

Find the distance the satellite travels in 95 minutes.

t A different satellite travels at a speed of 25\,200\,km/h.

Convert this speed into m/s.
